summary: Paul Graham argues that Yahoo’s decline stemmed from two early, compounding flaws: dependence on easy money from banner ads and startup ad spending, and an identity crisis that made it resist being a true technology company. These forces distracted leadership from the long-term value of search and undermined a hacker-centric culture, leading to mediocre software and a talent death spiral.
key_points: Yahoo’s banner-ad windfall and startup ad spending masked the real value of search and delayed strategic focus.
Yahoo’s self-image as a media company led to suit-centric management and undervaluing engineering.
Treating programming as a commodity produced weaker software and drove away top talent.
Hacker-centric culture is essential for any company that needs great software.
money_quotes: Yahoo had two problems Google didn't: easy money, and ambivalence about being a technology company.
As long as customers were writing big checks for banner ads, it was hard to take search seriously.
The worst consequence of trying to be a media company was that they didn't take programming seriously enough.
In technology, once you have bad programmers, you're doomed.
In the software business, you can't afford not to have a hacker-centric culture.
key_insight: Easy money and a suit-centric identity can blind a company to the need for a hacker-centric culture, and once technical excellence erodes, recovery is unlikely.
